The Quiet Power of Slow Reading

In an age of endless scrolling, slowing down has become a radical act. Slow reading is not a productivity hack; it is a way of paying attention.

When we read attentively, our brains build richer mental models of the text. We notice rhythm and metaphor, and we remember what we read days later instead of minutes later.

Researchers in cognitive science have repeatedly shown that comprehension improves when we re-read passages and pause between paragraphs. This is not because slow readers are smarter; it is because attention is the scarce resource of modern life.

So pick a book this evening, leave your phone in another room, and read for thirty minutes without interruption. The experience may feel strange at first, but the rewards compound quickly.
